title: Tether Hooks
desc: The work platforms rely on tether lines and nonmetal tools, and both have started drifting out of order.

steps.text:
At Iron Pillar, loose metal becomes a problem faster than most workers can form the sentence. That is why the platform stations use ceramic mallets, stone-faced gauges, fiber aprons, and tether hooks for anything that might otherwise leave a hand unexpectedly. The system works as long as each station remains stricter than the pillar's appetite. Today a few of the hooks have worn thin and the tool rack looks like someone trusted memory where labels would have been wiser.

The platform foreman kicks the rack leg into square and hands you a bundle of fresh clips. "Replace the tether hooks if you want the line work," she says. "Or sort the ceramic tools back onto the marked rack if you prefer the shelf. Either way, I'd like the next crew to lose less equipment to carelessness and magnetism."

options.label: Replace the worn tether hooks along the work line
Sort the ceramic and stone tools back onto the rack

steps.text:
The tether hooks clip tools, buckets, and sometimes entire belts to the fiber safety line that runs the length of the platform. Two have worn grooves deep enough to worry the foreman and another no longer springs shut cleanly. You unclip each bad hook, fit a fresh one, and test the catches until they bite the line with the firm little snap the station expects. Nothing glamorous about it. Just the difference between controlled risk and stupid risk.

The foreman loads one finished hook with a weighted bucket and gives it a hard yank toward the pillar. The line takes the strain. The bucket stays where it belongs. That seems to improve her opinion of the morning by a measurable amount. "Good. Let the metal pull all it wants. The hook can answer no."

options.label: Hang the old clips in the scrap pouch and clear the line

steps.text:
The tool rack is arranged by what will least annoy the field around it: ceramic hammers on the left, stone calipers above, resin-handled brushes below, fiber gauges and chalk tins in the outer row. After a long shift, that system has collapsed into a pile of almosts. You put it back in order piece by piece, returning every tool to the bracket that keeps it ready, legible, and safely boring.

By the time you finish, the rack looks disciplined again. No metal where metal should not be, no guessing where a needed gauge ought to hang. The foreman gives the shelf one approving glance. "Better. The pillar makes enough demands. The tool wall does not need ambitions of its own."

options.label: Step clear of the rack and leave the station sorted
